questions.questionText: In the equation F = kq1q2/r², what happens to the force if both charges are doubled?
questions.explanation: If q1 → 2q1 and q2 → 2q2, then F = k(2q1)(2q2)/r² = 4kq1q2/r² = 4F. The force becomes 4 times the original because charge appears as a product in the numerator.
hints: Both charges appear in the numerator

questions.questionText: Calculate the force between two charges of +2C and +3C separated by 1m. (k = 9 × 10^9 N·m²/C²)
questions.explanation: F = kq1q2/r² = (9 × 10^9)(2)(3)/(1)² = 9 × 10^9 × 6 = 54 × 10^9 N. This is an enormous force, showing why such large charges don't exist in everyday situations.

questions.questionText: The electrostatic force between two charges is 36N. If the distance is tripled, the new force will be:
questions.explanation: Since F ∝ 1/r², when r → 3r, the force becomes F/9. New force = 36/9 = 4N. The inverse square law means tripling the distance reduces force by a factor of 9.

questions.questionText: What is the dimension of Coulomb's constant k?
questions.explanation: From F = kq1q2/r², k = Fr²/(q1q2). Using dimensions: [M L T⁻²][L²]/[A T]² = [M L³ T⁻² A⁻² T⁻²] = [M L³ T⁻⁴ A⁻²]. This is the dimensional formula of Coulomb's constant.

questions.questionText: Coulomb's Law is similar in mathematical form to which other fundamental law?
questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law (F = kq1q2/r²) has the same mathematical form as Newton's Law of Gravitation (F = Gm1m2/r²). Both are inverse square laws, though they describe different fundamental forces.
